summary: The post argues that while AI-generated code looks messy to humans, it might actually be closer to “simple” than abstracted, human-friendly software. Drawing on Rich Hickey’s simple-vs-easy distinction, Stancil suggests that AI can handle massive volumes of duplicated, straightforward code better than we can, so fighting that tendency may be misguided. The provocative idea is to let AI build expansive, radically simple codebases that suit its strengths—even if they’re unaesthetic to us.
money_quotes: It is as though it’s an absent-minded architect who adds a new elevator for every floor in a skyscraper, because it forgot that there are already thirty elevators in the lobby.
In other words, AI writes code that is bad. Engineers (can) write code that is good.
The only way to avoid this eventual fate, Hickey says, is to keep things as simple as we can for as long as we can.
For computers, only complexity is hard. Volume is trivial. Volume is what computers do.

Perhaps we should let the machine write for itself and its abilities. Let it be redudant. Let it ignore the frameworks; let it create explosively large codebases. Let it write 1,000 distinct pipelines in 1,000 distinct languages. Let it be offensively unaesthetic. Let it be radically simple.
key_insight: AI’s strength is volume over abstraction, so a sprawling but simple, duplicated codebase may be more appropriate for machines than the elegant complexity humans prefer.
